team_building_principles.name: Speed control required
team_building_principles.guideline: Most doubles teams should include at least one way to control turn order: Tailwind, Trick Room, Icy Wind, Electroweb, Thunder Wave, Scary Face or similar.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Add reliable speed control so the team can choose when it moves first instead of relying only on raw Speed.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Clear win condition
team_building_principles.guideline: A good team should know what it is trying to enable: a Mega, endgame conversion pressure, weather abuser, Trick Room attacker, bulky setup piece or strong core.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Pick a primary win condition and use the other slots to support that plan.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: covering multiple team jobs
team_building_principles.guideline: Prefer Pokémon that cover more than one useful job, such as damage plus Fake Out, speed control plus disruption, or bulk plus redirection.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Add a role-compression Pokémon rather than another pure attacker.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Limit scarce enablers
team_building_principles.guideline: Team-defining enablers such as Trick Room, weather, Tailwind and screens are usually main roles, not moves to duplicate across half the team.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Keep one main setter for each scarce team role unless the team is deliberately built around redundancy.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Protect access
team_building_principles.guideline: Protect-style moves are important in doubles for stalling field effects, avoiding double-targets and positioning safely.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Consider adding Protect/Detect/Wide Guard/Quick Guard style protection to more selected builds.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Mixed offensive pressure
team_building_principles.guideline: Avoid being entirely physical or entirely special unless the strategy intentionally supports that; mixed pressure makes defensive answers harder.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Spread damage option
team_building_principles.guideline: Spread damage is valuable in doubles because it threatens both opposing slots and punishes passive turns.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Add at least one strong spread option such as Rock Slide, Heat Wave, Hyper Voice, Dazzling Gleam, Muddy Water, Earthquake or similar.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Disruption and denial
team_building_principles.guideline: Good teams often need ways to deny the opponent's plan, not just execute their own: Fake Out, Taunt, Encore, Snarl, Will-O-Wisp, Haze or Parting Shot style moves.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Add disruption so the team can stop opposing setup, speed control or endgame conversion pieces.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

team_building_principles.name: Emergency pressure
team_building_principles.guideline: Priority, Fake Out, bulk or disruption gives a team a way back when it loses the speed war.
team_building_principles.suggestion: Consider adding priority or Fake Out pressure as an emergency tempo tool.
team_building_principles.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

learning_concepts.title: Speed Control
learning_concepts.summary: Control turn order with Tailwind, Trick Room, paralysis, priority or speed drops.
learning_concepts.meaning: Speed control helps your team move before the opponent through effects like Tailwind, Trick Room, Icy Wind or paralysis.
learning_concepts.matters: Moving first often decides games. Fast teams keep momentum, while slower teams need ways to compensate.
mistakes: Mixing fast and slow Pokémon poorly
Relying on only one speed option
learning_concepts.checks: The builder checks average team speed, priority access, Tailwind users, Trick Room setters and speed-lowering support.
learning_concepts.confidenceReason: Strategic/analysis metadata preserved as experimental; not official Pokémon Champions battle data.

learning_concepts.matters: Weather can increase damage, activate abilities and shape entire matchups.
supports: Weather abusers like Swift Swim or Chlorophyll

learning_concepts.meaning: Trick Room reverses move order so slower Pokémon act first.
learning_concepts.matters: It allows bulky, powerful Pokémon to overwhelm faster offensive teams.
mistakes: Using Pokémon that are too fast
No protection for Trick Room setup
Weak backup mode outside Trick Room
learning_concepts.checks: The builder checks team speed balance, Trick Room setters, slow attackers and support options that help setup safely.

archetypes.name: Trick Room
archetypes.core_strategy: Use Trick Room to reverse normal Speed order and let slow, powerful attackers move first. The team usually protects the setter early, then converts the limited Trick Room turns into KOs or heavy board control.
archetypes.win_condition: Set Trick Room safely, then trade aggressively with slow attackers before the room expires.
recognition_cues: Trick Room setter plus very slow heavy attacker on team preview
Protect or Fake Out support positioned around the setter
Several attackers that prefer moving last under normal Speed order

archetypes.name: Sun
archetypes.core_strategy: Sun teams use Drought or manual sun to boost Fire pressure and enable fast Grass or solar-based attackers. They aim to create turns where Fire attacks, Chlorophyll-style speed, and weather-boosted coverage are hard to switch into.
archetypes.win_condition: Keep sun active long enough for boosted attackers to overwhelm shared Fire, Grass, or speed weaknesses.
recognition_cues: Mega Charizard Y or Torkoal on preview
Fire attackers paired with Grass attackers
Manual speed control or redirection supporting sun turns

archetypes.name: Rain
archetypes.core_strategy: Rain teams use Drizzle or manual rain to boost Water damage and reduce incoming Fire damage. They often combine fast Water pressure with pivots, Steel-types, or Electric coverage that benefits from rain positioning.
archetypes.win_condition: Maintain rain and use repeated Water pressure to force defensive trades until the opponent runs out of safe switches.
recognition_cues: Pelipper or Politoed on preview
Multiple Water attackers or rain-boosted Weather Ball users
Steel or Electric partners protected by rain’s Fire reduction

archetypes.name: Snow/Hail + Aurora Veil
archetypes.core_strategy: Snow teams use snow setters and Aurora Veil to make the board harder to remove. They win longer trades by combining Blizzard pressure, safer setup, and bulky attackers behind Veil.
archetypes.win_condition: Set snow and Aurora Veil, then use the defensive buffer to win repeated trades.
recognition_cues: Alolan Ninetales, Mega Froslass, or Abomasnow on preview
Aurora Veil plus Blizzard users
Bulky attackers that become difficult to remove behind screens

archetypes.name: Sand
archetypes.core_strategy: Sand teams use sand setters to enable Rock, Ground, and Steel pressure while adding chip damage over time. They usually pair a durable weather setter with a fast sand abuser or bulky attacker that benefits from matchup compression.
archetypes.win_condition: Keep sand active while forcing repeated chip and positioning advantages with Ground, Rock, or Steel attackers.
recognition_cues: Tyranitar or Hippowdon on preview
Excadrill next to sand support
Multiple Ground, Rock, or Steel attackers

archetypes.name: Hyper Offense
archetypes.core_strategy: Hyper Offense stacks fast threats and immediate damage so the opponent has little time to stabilize. It accepts some defensive risk in exchange for forcing KOs, speed pressure, or snowball turns from the start.
archetypes.win_condition: Take early KOs and keep tempo high enough that the opponent cannot reset positioning.
recognition_cues: Multiple very fast attackers
Little defensive overlap
Tailwind, priority, or immediate spread damage support

archetypes.name: Bulky Offense
archetypes.core_strategy: Bulky Offense uses durable attackers that can take hits while still threatening meaningful damage. It wins by trading efficiently, forcing awkward targets, and keeping enough board presence to avoid falling behind.
archetypes.win_condition: Outlast the opponent’s first wave of pressure and convert with high-value attackers.
recognition_cues: Several mid-speed bulky attackers
Incineroar-style pivot support
Flexible defensive typing rather than one all-in mode

archetypes.name: Balance
archetypes.core_strategy: Balance teams combine offence, defensive pivots, speed control, and matchup tools instead of relying on one mode. They adapt their lead and backline to the opponent’s preview.
archetypes.win_condition: Use flexible positioning to create favourable trades until one attacker can safely clean up.
recognition_cues: One or two speed-control options
A mix of bulky pivots and offensive threats
No single weather or room dependency

archetypes.name: Tailwind Offense
archetypes.core_strategy: Tailwind Offense uses temporary speed control to make strong attackers move first for several turns. It usually leads a Tailwind setter beside an immediate threat, then pressures KOs before Tailwind expires.
archetypes.win_condition: Set Tailwind and convert the speed advantage into two or more decisive KOs.
recognition_cues: Whimsicott, Talonflame, or Corviknight on preview
Fast attackers that become overwhelming under Tailwind
Protect-heavy turns around Tailwind duration

archetypes.name: Perish Trap
archetypes.core_strategy: Perish Trap uses trapping, defensive cycling, and Perish Song pressure to remove Pokémon without direct damage races. It focuses on denying switches, surviving turns, and forcing the opponent into losing board states.
archetypes.win_condition: Trap key targets, start a Perish count, and survive until the trapped Pokémon faint.
recognition_cues: Mega Gengar with bulky partners
Perish Song users plus Protect cycling
Low immediate damage but strong denial tools

archetypes.name: Redirection
archetypes.core_strategy: Redirection teams use Follow Me or Rage Powder support to protect fragile attackers, setup users, or Trick Room setters. Their pressure comes from forcing the opponent to hit the wrong target while the real threat gains tempo.
archetypes.win_condition: Redirect key attacks long enough for a partner to set up, attack freely, or control the board.
recognition_cues: Clefable, Venusaur, or Alcremie next to a setup attacker
Protect plus redirection loops
One obvious protected win-condition Pokémon

archetypes.name: Screens Offense
archetypes.core_strategy: Screens Offense uses Reflect, Light Screen, or Aurora Veil to reduce incoming damage while attackers trade aggressively. It sits between offence and bulk, using temporary defensive turns to enable safer attacks or setup.
archetypes.win_condition: Establish screens early and use the damage reduction to win trades before the screen turns expire.
recognition_cues: Dedicated screen setter on preview
Bulky setup or mid-speed attackers behind support
Aurora Veil or dual screens as the main defensive layer

archetypes.name: Setup Sweep
archetypes.core_strategy: Setup Sweep teams dedicate resources to giving one or two Pokémon safe turns to boost. They often use redirection, screens, Fake Out, or defensive pivots to create the setup window.
archetypes.win_condition: Boost safely, then use the boosted Pokémon to take repeated KOs before the opponent can reset it.
recognition_cues: Dragon Dance, Quiver Dance, Belly Drum, or similar setup threat
Redirection or screens beside the setup user
Team structure heavily protecting one sweeper

archetypes.name: Weatherless Goodstuffs
archetypes.core_strategy: Weatherless Goodstuffs uses individually strong Pokémon with flexible matchups instead of committing to weather, Trick Room, or a single setup mode. The team wins through strong leads, pivots, type coverage, and consistent damage across many matchups.
archetypes.win_condition: Use high-value Pokémon to win positioning and trade better than the opponent across the full game.
recognition_cues: No obvious weather, room, or setup dependency
Several splashable high-value Pokémon
Flexible lead options with strong defensive overlap
